---
title: "Extended F4"
output: html_document
date: "2026-05-03"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(cowplot)
```

```{r}
# Read the dietary data
# The color key file is omitted here because the final plot relies only on the food descriptions
dtb <- read_csv('../data/152_combined_DTB.csv') |> mutate(Food_code = as.character(Food_code))
```

# d:top 10 food codes in sweets

```{r}
# Isolate the top 10 most consumed items in the Sweets/Beverages category (food group starting with '9')
# This sums up both the total dehydrated weight and the sugar content across the entire dataset
top_sweets <- dtb |>
  mutate(fgrp1 = str_sub(Food_code, 1, 1)) |>
  filter(fgrp1 == '9') |>
  group_by(Food_code, description) |>
  summarise(
    total_per_code = sum(dehydrated_weight, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_sugar_per_code = sum(Sugars_g, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  slice_max(order_by = total_per_code, n = 10)

# Calculate the frequency of consumption for these specific items
# Counting distinct meals ensures the average reflects a true "per-sitting" portion size
meal_counts <- dtb |>
  filter(Food_code %in% top_sweets$Food_code) |>
  distinct(Food_code, pid, fdrt, Meal) |>
  count(Food_code, name = "meal_n")
```


```{r}
# Merge the totals with the meal counts to calculate the effective average,
# then format the data into a long format required for grouped bar charts
plot_data <- top_sweets |>
  inner_join(meal_counts, by = "Food_code") |>
  mutate(
    `Total` = total_per_code / meal_n,
    `Sugar content` = total_sugar_per_code / meal_n
  ) |>
  select(description, `Total`, `Sugar content`) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(`Total`, `Sugar content`),
    names_to = "metric",
    values_to = "grams"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    metric = factor(metric, levels = c("Sugar content", "Total")),
    # Hardcode the exact string formats to match the publication figure perfectly.
    # This prevents awkward automatic wrapping.
    description = case_when(
      str_detect(str_to_lower(description), "plus") ~ "Nutritional drink or shake,\nready-to-drink (high calorie)",
      str_detect(str_to_lower(description), "ready-to-drink") ~ "Nutritional drink or shake,\nready-to-drink",
      str_detect(str_to_lower(description), "enteral") ~ "Enteral (tube feed) formula",
      str_detect(str_to_lower(description), "sports drink") ~ "Sports drink",
      TRUE ~ str_wrap(description, width = 35)
    )
  )

# Extract the correct y-axis ordering based purely on the Total grams
y_axis_order <- plot_data |>
  filter(metric == "Total") |>
  arrange(grams) |>
  pull(description)

# Apply the ordering back to the main dataset
plot_data <- plot_data |>
  mutate(description = factor(description, levels = y_axis_order))

# Build the plot using pure ggplot2 integrated with cowplot for a clean, academic look
fig_e4d <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = grams, y = description, fill = metric)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Sugar content" = "#ffbcdc", "Total" = "#db2589")) +
  labs(
    x = "Effective per-meal\naverage consumption\nin dehydrated weight (grams)",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    legend.position = c(0.75, 0.4), # Places the legend inside the plot area, mimicking panel d
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.line.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_line(color = "grey85", linetype = "dashed")
  )

fig_e4d

# Save the plot with matching dimensions
ggsave(
  '../data/E04d_R62_top10_sweets.pdf', 
  plot = fig_e4d, 
  width = 80, 
  height = 90, 
  units = "mm", 
  device = 'pdf'
)
```
